title: What is Digital Falls Screening?
description: Digital falls screening explained. How electronic platforms standardize falls risk assessment with validated questionnaires, real-time data capture, and automated risk scoring.
featuredImageAlt: Older woman using digital screening tool at home

excerpt: Digital falls screening standardizes risk assessment with validated tools, real-time data capture, and automated scoring. Learn how it compares to paper-based methods.

blocks.heading: Digital falls screening transforms risk assessment.
blocks.bodyText: See how Able Assess structures screening workflows for your care setting.

body:
## What is digital falls screening?

Digital falls screening is the use of electronic platforms to conduct systematic fall risk assessments. Instead of paper forms, clipboards and manual scoring, a digital screening tool guides the assessor through a validated protocol, captures data in real time and generates a risk score automatically.

The concept is straightforward. Falls are the leading cause of injury-related hospital admissions among older adults. Screening identifies who is at elevated risk before a fall occurs. The challenge has always been consistency. Paper-based screening relies on individual clinicians to select the right tool, administer it correctly, score it accurately and record the results in a retrievable format. Each of those steps introduces variability.

Digital screening removes that variability. The platform enforces a standardized workflow. Every assessor follows the same protocol, in the same order, with the same scoring logic. The data is captured at the point of care and stored in a structured format that supports longitudinal tracking, reporting and integration with electronic health records.

Able Assess is a purpose-built digital falls screening platform. It combines validated questionnaires with objective physical measurements, including grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go. The result is a multifactorial risk profile generated in under five minutes by clinical or non-clinical staff.

For care operators, commissioners and clinical leads, digital screening represents a shift from subjective judgment to standardized, evidence-based assessment at scale.

## Digital screening vs paper-based methods

The differences between digital and paper-based falls screening are significant across five dimensions.

### Inter-rater reliability

Paper-based screening depends on individual interpretation. Two assessors using the same paper form may score differently based on how they read instructions, observe performance or interpret thresholds. Studies consistently show moderate inter-rater reliability for paper-based functional assessments.

Digital platforms enforce consistency. The protocol is fixed. Scoring is automatic. Two assessors using Able Assess with the same individual will produce the same risk classification because the system removes interpretive variability from the process.

### Data capture completeness

Paper forms frequently have missing fields. A 2019 audit of paper-based falls screening in NHS trusts found that 34% of forms had at least one incomplete section. Missing data undermines the validity of the screening and creates gaps in the clinical record.

Digital platforms require completion of each field before progressing. Data capture completeness in Able Assess exceeds 99% because the workflow does not allow fields to be skipped.

### Time efficiency

Paper-based screening involves the assessment itself, manual scoring, transcription into a care record and filing the physical form. Total time per screening typically ranges from 15 to 25 minutes.

Digital screening consolidates these steps. Able Assess completes the assessment, scores it and stores the structured data in a single workflow. Total time is under five minutes for a four-metric multifactorial screening.

### Data accuracy

Manual transcription introduces errors. A digit transposed, a score miscalculated, a form misfiled. These errors are difficult to detect and can result in incorrect risk classification.

Digital capture eliminates transcription. The data recorded is the data measured. Scoring algorithms apply validated cut-offs consistently, every time.

### Longitudinal tracking

Paper records make it difficult to track change over time. Comparing a screening result from six months ago with today's result requires locating the original form, interpreting handwritten notes and manually calculating the difference.

Digital platforms store every screening in a structured database. Able Assess displays longitudinal trends automatically, making it straightforward to identify decline, measure intervention effectiveness and report outcomes to commissioners or regulators.

## Why Able Assess for digital screening

Not all digital tools are equivalent. Many electronic health record systems include basic screening questionnaires, but these are typically self-report tools without objective measurement. Able Assess is different because it was purpose-built for falls screening.

### Validated protocols

Able Assess implements screening protocols exactly as they were validated in research. The platform supports CDC STEADI, NICE 2025 guidance and multifactorial approaches recommended by the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention.

### Objective measurement

The platform combines questionnaire-based screening with objective physical measurement using the GripAble sensor. Grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go are captured digitally, scored against normative data and integrated into a single risk profile.

### Multidisciplinary access

Screening data is accessible to the full care team. Physical therapists, occupational therapists, primary care physicians, care managers and commissioners can all view results through the Able Assess portal. This supports coordinated intervention planning.

### Quality assurance

Every screening is time-stamped, attributed to an assessor and stored with full audit trail. For organizations subject to CQC, Joint Commission or state survey requirements, this provides defensible evidence of systematic screening.

### Scalability

Able Assess scales from a single care home to a network of 10,000 individuals. The same workflow, the same data format and the same reporting structure apply whether you are screening 100 people or 10,000.

## Frequently asked questions

### What screening protocols does Able Assess support?

Able Assess supports CDC STEADI, NICE 2025 falls guidance and multifactorial screening protocols aligned with the 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention. The platform implements these protocols exactly as validated, ensuring that screening results are clinically defensible and comparable across sites and time periods.

### How long does a digital assessment take?

A full multifactorial screening with Able Assess takes under five minutes. This includes grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed and Timed Up and Go. The guided workflow means no additional time is needed for scoring, interpretation or data entry. Paper-based equivalents typically take 15 to 25 minutes.

### Can it integrate with our EHR?

Yes. Able Assess supports integration with major electronic health record systems through standard data exchange protocols. Screening results can be exported in structured formats compatible with OASIS, Section GG and other regulatory reporting requirements. Contact the Able Care team for details on specific EHR integrations.

### What happens after screening?

After screening, Able Assess generates a risk classification and highlights specific risk factors identified during the assessment. This information supports targeted intervention planning. The platform provides recommendations aligned with evidence-based guidelines and enables referral to appropriate services such as physical therapy, medication review or home modification.

### Does digital screening replace clinical judgment?

No. Digital screening standardizes the data collection and scoring process. It ensures that every individual receives a consistent, evidence-based assessment. Clinical judgment remains essential for interpreting results in context, planning interventions and making care decisions. The platform provides clinicians with better data, not a replacement for their expertise.
